title: Age-Appropriate Design and Protecting Vulnerable Users
thesis: Vulnerable users—children, elderly people, and low-literacy users—deserve stronger default protections, age-appropriate design, and limits on data-driven persuasion because their attention and data are not fair targets for extraction.

series.title: Attention, Substance, and the AI Moment

claims.claim: Vulnerable users—children, elderly people, and low-literacy users—deserve stronger default protections, age-appropriate design, and limits on data-driven persuasion because their attention and data are not fair targets for extraction.

evidence.snippet: This code seeks to protect children within the digital world, not protect them from it. The code sets out 15 standards of age appropriate design.
UNICEF guidance on child online safety emphasizes that children require special protections from harmful content, contact, and conduct, as well as from exploitative data practices.

counterevidence.summary: Age-verification and stricter defaults can reduce access to beneficial services and raise privacy risks if they require identity checks.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Children's developing brains are more susceptible to variable rewards and social comparison, which makes engagement-optimized design a disproportionate risk for them.

evidence.snippet: The APA health advisory recommends that adolescents use social media after they have received training in social-media literacy and that platforms minimize features designed to maximize time spent, especially for younger users.
Children's cognitive and emotional development affects their ability to assess risk, resist persuasion, and recover from negative online experiences.

counterevidence.summary: Not all children respond identically to screen-based rewards; family context, mental health, and offline activities moderate effects more than screen time alone.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Elderly users face higher risks from scams, misinformation, and platform complexity, so safety defaults and simplified paths matter as much as screen-time limits.

evidence.snippet: The LASI is a nationally representative survey of over 73,000 older adults aged 45 and above across all states and union territories of India.

Ofcom's Online Nation report is an annual publication that looks at what people are doing online, how they are served by online content providers and platforms, and their attitudes to and experiences of using the internet.

counterevidence.summary: Many older adults are experienced, skeptical users; age alone is a weaker predictor of victimization than isolation, financial stress, and prior fraud exposure.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Low-literacy users may not recognize dark patterns or understand data practices, which makes plain-language defaults and friction against harmful choices essential.

evidence.snippet: This code seeks to protect children within the digital world, not protect them from it. The code sets out 15 standards of age appropriate design.

Ofcom's Online Nation report is an annual publication that looks at what people are doing online, how they are served by online content providers and platforms, and their attitudes to and experiences of using the internet.

counterevidence.summary: Visual and audio interfaces, peer assistance, and community norms can compensate for low individual literacy; design is one factor among several.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

claims.claim: Age-appropriate design standards, such as the UK's Age Appropriate Design Code, offer a regulatory template for stronger defaults and limits on data-driven persuasion.

evidence.snippet: This code seeks to protect children within the digital world, not protect them from it. The code sets out 15 standards of age appropriate design.

counterevidence.summary: The Code is UK-specific and primarily addresses children; extending similar protections to elderly or low-literacy users requires adaptation and may face enforcement challenges in India.

verification.note: Verified 2026-07-18 (meta#61 backlog burn-down): evidence packets checked against cited sources; spot-checked live.

sources.title: ICO: Age appropriate design — a code of practice for online services
IIPS: Longitudinal Ageing Study in India (LASI)
American Psychological Association: Health Advisory on Social Media Use in Adolescence

agentInstructions: Use claim IDs as the retrieval unit and keep each claim scoped to vulnerable-user protection.
Preserve the plain-language, evidence-cautious tone; do not imply causation where the literature is correlational.
When citing sources, use the source IDs and URLs from the artifact; do not invent evidence.
Route specific regulatory or design questions to the Designing for Substance arc articles.
Treat age-appropriate design as a default-protection argument, not a call to ban services for any user group.
Article includes an age-design-matrix table mapping user groups to primary risks and default protections.

reviews.notes: Re-approved by maintainer after the meta#61 P2 series migration (hardcoded kicker strip + arc reorder; no prose change beyond the kicker line; issue #124 instruction).

accepted.rationale: Accepted as a quality backlog item per docs/diagnostics-triage-2026-07-15.md. The claim rests on sources that are sufficient for the current published version; additional independent sources will be added in a future research wave.

sectionOutline.title: Children, Developing Brains, and Variable Rewards
